Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمُدَّثِّرُ (١)
O you wrapped up in your mantle! (1)
قُمْ فَأَنذِرْ (٢)
Rise up and warn! (2)
وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ (٣)
Magnify your Lord, (3)
وَثِيَابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ (٤)
and purify your clothes, (4)
وَٱلرُّجْزَ فَٱهْجُرْ (٥)
and keep away from all impurity! (5)
وَلَا تَمْنُن تَسْتَكْثِرُ (٦)
Do not grant a favour seeking a greater gain, (6)
وَلِرَبِّكَ فَٱصْبِرْ (٧)
and be patient for the sake of your Lord. (7)
فَإِذَا نُقِرَ فِى ٱلنَّاقُورِ (٨)
When the Trumpet will be sounded, (8)
فَذَٰلِكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوْمٌ عَسِيرٌ (٩)
that will be a day of hardship, (9)
عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ غَيْرُ يَسِيرٍ (١٠)
not at all easy for the faithless. (10)

Content of Surah "Al-Muddaththir"

There is no doubt that this Surah is among those revealed in Makkah. However, there is a discussion regarding whether it was the very first Surah revealed to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) or if it was revealed after Surah "Al-Alaq" (Iqra).

A careful examination of the contents of Surah Al-Alaq and Surah Al-Muddaththir indicates that "Iqra" was at the very beginning of the Call, while Surah Al-Muddaththir pertains to the time when the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) was commanded to commence the public invitation and the period of the secret Call had ended. Therefore, some have stated that Surah Al-Alaq is the first Surah revealed at the start of the Prophetic Mission (Be'that), and Surah Al-Muddaththir is the first Surah after the public Call began; this seems to be a sound reconciliation of the views.

In any case, the nature of Meccan Surahs—which involve the call to the Origin (Monotheism) and the Resurrection, the struggle against polytheism, and warning and threatening the opponents with Divine punishment—is fully reflected in this Surah.

The discussions in this Surah generally revolve around seven axes:

  1. The call to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to rise, warn, and deliver the public message, and to exercise patience and steadfastness on this path, while acquiring the necessary readiness for this task.

  2. Reference to the Resurrection and the characteristics of the people of Hell—those who rose to oppose the Quran and took to mocking the Truth.

  3. A portion of the features of Hell coupled with the warning to the disbelievers.

  4. Emphasis on the matter of Resurrection through repeated oaths.

  5. The connection between the destiny of every human being and their deeds, and the rejection of any illogical ideas in this regard.

  6. A portion of the characteristics of the people of Paradise and the people of Hell, and the fate of each group.

  7. The manner in which the ignorant, unaware, arrogant, and selfish individuals flee from the Truth.

Virtue of Reciting the Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), it is stated:

مَنْ قَرَأَ سُورَةَ الْمُدَّثِّرِ أُعْطِيَ مِنَ الْأَجْرِ عَشْرَ حَسَنَاتٍ بِعَدَدِ مَنْ صَدَّقَ بِمُحَمَّدٍ (ص) وَ كَذَّبَ بِهِ بِمَكَّةَ

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Muddaththir will be given ten good deeds for every person who either believed in or disbelieved in the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) in Makkah." [^1] And in another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him), it is mentioned:

مَنْ قَرَأَ فِي الْفَرِيضَةِ سُورَةَ الْمُدَّثِّرِ كَانَ حَقّاً عَلَى اللَّهِ أَنْ يَجْعَلَهُ مَعَ مُحَمَّدٍ (ص) فِي دَرَجَتِهِ، وَ لَا يُدْرِكُهُ فِي حَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا شَقَاءٌ أَبَداً

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Muddaththir in an obligatory prayer, it is a right upon Allah that He places him with the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) in his rank and proximity, and misery and hardship shall never overtake him in the life of this world." [^2] It is obvious that such magnificent results will not follow from merely reciting the words of the Surah; rather, one must also consider the content of the Surah and implement it meticulously.

[^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 383.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ (1) قُمْ فَأَنْذِرْ (2) وَ رَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ (3) وَ ثِيابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ (4) وَ الرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ (5) وَ لا تَمْنُنْ تَسْتَكْثِرُ (6) وَ لِرَبِّكَ فَاصْبِرْ (7) فَإِذا نُقِرَ فِي النَّاقُورِ (8) فَذلِكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوْمٌ عَسِيرٌ (9) عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ غَيْرُ يَسِيرٍ (10)

Commentary

Arise and Warn the Worlds!

Undoubtedly, the addressee in these verses is the person of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), even though there is no explicit mention of this title within them; however, the existing context in these verses manifests this reality.

First, He says: "O you enveloped in your garments!" (يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ).

"Arise and warn [the people of the worlds]!" (قُمْ فَأَنْذِرْ). For the time of sleep and rest has passed, and the time for rising and preaching has arrived.

The emphasis specifically on "warning" (Inzar), despite the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) being both a "bringer of glad tidings" (Bashir) and a "warner" (Nazir), is because "warning" has a deeper impact, especially at the beginning of the work, in awakening sleeping souls.

Regarding why the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) was resting in bed when this address called him to rise, commentators have offered several possibilities:

  1. The Arab polytheists gathered on the eve of the Hajj season, and their leaders—such as Abu Jahl, Abu Sufyan, Walid ibn al-Mughirah, Nadr ibn al-Harith, and others—consulted on what to say in response to the questions of people coming to Makkah from outside who had heard bits and pieces about the emergence of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family). If each were to give a different answer—one calling him a soothsayer, another a madman, and another a sorcerer—this divergence of opinions would have a negative effect. They felt they must rise in a propaganda campaign against the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) with a unified word! After discussion, they concluded that the best thing was to say he is a "sorcerer" (Sahir), because one of the effects of the phenomenon of "sorcery" is creating separation between spouses, and between father and child, and the Prophet had done such a thing by presenting the religion of Islam! This talk reached the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family); he became deeply distressed and, feeling ill and sorrowful, came home and lay in bed, whereupon the above verses were revealed, calling him to rise and struggle.

  2. These verses were among the very first revealed to the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), as it is narrated from Jabir ibn Abdullah that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: "I was on Mount Hira when a voice rose saying, 'O Muhammad! You are the Messenger of Allah!' I looked to the right and left and saw nothing. I looked above my head and saw an angel on a throne between the heavens and the earth. I became afraid and returned to Khadijah and said: 'Cover me! Cover me! And pour cold water on me.' It was at this moment that Gabriel descended and brought 'O you enveloped in your garments!'" However, considering that the verses of the Surah indicate a public call, it becomes certain that these verses were revealed at least after three years of secret invitation, and this does not align with what is mentioned in the above narration, unless it is said that the first few verses of this Surah were revealed at the start of the Call, and the subsequent verses pertain to several years later!

  3. The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) was asleep and had cast a garment over himself; Gabriel descended, woke him, and recited these verses to him, telling him to arise, set aside the bed and sleep, and attend to the fulfillment of the Mission.

  4. The "enveloping in garments" does not refer to physical clothing, but rather the raiment of Prophethood and Messengership, just as "the raiment of righteousness" (Libas al-Taqwa) is mentioned regarding piety.

  5. "Muddaththir" refers to one who has taken to a corner of seclusion and lives in isolation and solitude. Thus, the verse says: come out of isolation and seclusion, and attend to warning the creation and guiding the servants of Allah. [^1]

The first interpretation seems the most appropriate among these five.

It is noteworthy that the phrase "warn" (fa-anzir) does not specify about what or which subject he should warn; this is actually to express generality. That is, warn the people about idolatry, polytheism, disbelief, oppression, awakening, and corruption; warn them about Divine punishment, the reckoning of the Gathering, and so on (as the omission of the object indicates generality). Furthermore, it includes the punishment of this world, the punishment of the Hereafter, and the evil consequences of human actions that will overtake them.

Following the call to rise and warn, He gives five important instructions to the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), which serve as a model for others. The first instruction concerns Monotheism (Tawhid); He says: "And your Lord [alone] magnify!" (وَ رَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ) [^1]—the same God who is your Owner and Nourisher, and from Whom you have everything you possess. Consign all else to oblivion, draw a red line through all false deities, and erase all traces of polytheism and idol worship.

The emphasis on the word "Lord" (Rabb) and placing it before "magnify" (kabbir)—which indicates exclusivity—presents the issue of Monotheism with its reasoning in this short phrase. How beautiful and profound are the expressions of the Quran, that so much meaning is expressed in such a brief statement.

The meaning of the phrase "magnify" (fa-kabbir) is not merely saying "Allahu Akbar," although saying "Allahu Akbar" is one of its instances mentioned in narrations. Rather, the meaning is to consider your God as Great, both in belief, in action, and in speech, and to attribute to Him the qualities of beauty and deem Him far above any defect or flaw. Indeed, consider Him greater than what can be contained in description, just as it has come in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) that the meaning of "Allahu Akbar" is that Allah is greater than being described or being contained within human thought. Therefore, "Takbir" (magnification) has a broader concept than "Tasbih" (glorification), which only includes sanctification from any flaw or defect.

Following the issue of Monotheism, the second instruction is given regarding purity from defilements; He adds: "And your garments purify!" (وَ ثِيابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ). The reference to "garments" may be a metaphor for human actions, for the deeds of every person are like his garments, and his outward state reflects his inward state.

Some have also said that "garments" here refers to the heart, soul, and spirit; meaning, purify your heart from every defilement. Where the garment must be purified, the owner of the garment has priority.

Others have interpreted it as the literal outward clothing, since the cleanliness of outward clothing is among the most important signs of a person's character, upbringing, and culture. Especially in the age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), they rarely avoided impurities and had very soiled clothes. In particular, it was customary (as is also common among those caught in the modern age of ignorance) to make the hems of the garments very long, such that they dragged on the ground and became defiled. The fact that it is narrated in some traditions from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said the meaning of the verse is "shorten your garments" [^1] also points to this same meaning.

Some have also interpreted it as "spouses," since the Quran says: "They are a garment for you, and you are a garment for them" (هُنَّ لِباسٌ لَكُمْ وَ أَنْتُمْ لِباسٌ لَهُنَّ - Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 187), as you protect each other's honor and are ornaments for one another.

Combining these meanings is also possible. In reality, the verse also points to the fact that Divine leaders can only have influence when their own skirts are clean of any defilement and their piety and righteousness are certain from every aspect. Therefore, following the command to rise and warn, He gives the command for purity.

In the third instruction, He says: "And avoid the 'Rujz' (abomination) and that which causes Divine punishment!" (وَ الرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ). The breadth of the concept of "Rujz" (defilement/abomination) has led to various interpretations being mentioned for it.

Sometimes it is interpreted as idols, sometimes as every kind of sin, sometimes as ugly and undesirable morals, sometimes as the love of the world—which is the beginning of every mistake and sin—sometimes as the Divine punishment which is the result of polytheism and sin, and sometimes as anything that makes a person heedless of Allah.

The main point is that "Rujz" originally means "agitation and instability," [^1] and subsequently, it has been applied to every sin, polytheism, idol worship, satanic whispers, vile morals, and Divine punishment, all of which cause human agitation and divert one from the correct path.

While some consider the meaning of this word to be "punishment," [^2] since polytheism, sin, bad morals, and love of the world attract Divine punishment, they are also called "Rujz." It is also worth mentioning that in the Holy Quran, the word "Rijz" (with a different vowel) mostly appears with the meaning of punishment. [^1] Some also believe that "Rujz" and "Rijs"—which means "defilement"—are synonymous. [^2] Although these three meanings differ, they are closely related. In any case, the verse has a comprehensive concept that includes every kind of deviation, ugly and defiled act, and every deed that causes Divine wrath and punishment in this world and the Hereafter.

It is certain that the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) avoided and shunned these matters even before his Prophethood, and the history of his life—to which friend and foe alike testify—is proof of this. However, here it is emphasized as a fundamental principle in the path of calling towards Allah, and also as a model and example for everyone.

In the fourth instruction, He says: "And do not confer favor, seeking to acquire more" (وَ لا تَمْنُنْ تَسْتَكْثِرُ). [^3] Regarding what cases the prohibition of conferring favor and seeking increase applies to, the concept of the verse here is again general and broad.

It includes every kind of conferring of favor upon the Creator and the creation. Do not confer favor upon your Lord that you are performing Jihad and striving for Him, for He has conferred favor upon you by granting you this exalted station.

Likewise, do not consider your worship, obedience, and righteous deeds as "much"; rather, always see yourself at the limit of "shortcoming" and "deficiency," and consider worship as a kind of great Divine success (Tawfiq) for yourself.

In other words, you must not consider your rising and warning, the spreading of Monotheism, the explanation of the Lord's greatness, the purification of garments, and the avoidance of every sin as a basis for conferring favor upon Allah, nor should you consider them great. Rather, you must see these as precious gifts from your Lord and be grateful for them, and be so drowned in His love and affection that you consider these important tasks as very insignificant.

Furthermore, if you perform a service for the creation—whether in spiritual matters like preaching and guidance, or in material matters like spending and giving—none of it should be coupled with conferring favor or the expectation of compensation, especially a greater compensation. For conferring favor (Mann) renders good deeds void and ineffective: "O you who believe! Do not void your charities with reminders [of your generosity] and injury" (Surah Al-Baqarah: 264).

"La tamnun" from the root "Mann" in such cases refers to speech or words that express the importance of a blessing one has given to another. From here, its relationship with the issue of "Istikthar" (seeking increase) becomes clear; for if a person considers his service insignificant, he does not expect a reward, let alone seeking an increase. Thus, conferring favor is always the source of "Istikthar"—an act that completely destroys the value of the blessing.

The fact that it is mentioned in some narrations that the meaning of the verse is: "Do not give something to another while expecting more than it in return" [^1] is, in fact, an explanation of one of the branches of the general concept of the noble verse.

Just as it is mentioned in another Hadith that Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said in the interpretation of this verse: "Never consider the good you have done for Allah as much" [^2]—this is also another branch of that general concept.

In the following verse, He points to the final instruction in this regard, saying: "And for your Lord, be patient" (وَ لِرَبِّكَ فَاصْبِرْ). Here again, we encounter a broad concept of "patience, steadfastness, and endurance" that encompasses everything.

That is, be patient in the path of fulfilling this great Mission; be patient against the harassment of the polytheists and the ignorant enemies; be steadfast in the path of servitude and obeying Allah's command; and be patient in the struggle against the self (Nafs) and in the field of Jihad against the enemy.

It is certain that patience and steadfastness are the foundation and the guarantee for the implementation of all previous programs. Fundamentally, the most important asset in the path of preaching and guidance is this very patience and steadfastness. Therefore, it has been emphasized many times in the Holy Quran. For this reason, we read in the words of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him): "Patience and steadfastness are to faith as the head is to the body!" [^3] And also for this reason, one of the most important programs of the Prophets and men of God was this program of patience and steadfastness; the more severe and heavy the events that fell upon them, the greater their patience became.

In a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), we read regarding the reward of the patient: "Allah the Exalted says: 'When I direct a calamity toward one of My servants in his body, his wealth, or his child, and he then meets that with beautiful patience, I am ashamed before him on the Day of Resurrection to set up a scale for him or to open a record for him!'" [^1]

Following the instruction regarding rising and warning in the previous verses, in the verses under discussion, He begins the warning with a very emphatic and eloquent expression, saying: "For when the trumpet is sounded" (فَإِذا نُقِرَ فِي النَّاقُورِ).

"That day, then, will be a difficult day" (فَذلِكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوْمٌ عَسِيرٌ). [^2]

"A day of great hardship, not easy for the disbelievers" (عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ غَيْرُ يَسِيرٍ).

It is noteworthy that "Naqur" is originally from the root "Naqr," meaning a striking that leads to piercing. The "beak" (Minqar) of birds, which is a tool for striking and piercing objects, is also derived from this meaning. For this reason, a trumpet whose sound seemingly pierces the human ear and penetrates the brain is called "Naqur."

It is well understood from the verses of the Quran that at the end of the world and the beginning of the Resurrection, the Trumpet (Sur) will be blown twice. That is, two extraordinarily terrifying and shaking sounds will encompass the entire world: the first is the sound of death, and the second is the sound of awakening and life. These are referred to as the "first blowing of the Trumpet" and the "second blowing of the Trumpet." The verse under discussion refers to the "second blowing," by which the Resurrection is established, and it is a difficult and heavy day for the disbelievers.

Regarding the "Trumpet" (Sur) and the "Blowing of the Trumpet," we have had a detailed discussion under verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar (Vol. 9, pp. 534 to 542).

In any case, the above verses reflect the reality that upon the blowing of the Resurrection, the problems of the disbelievers will appear one after another. It is a very painful, calamitous, and unbearable day that will bring the most powerful of men to their knees.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr Razi, Vol. 30, pp. 189-190. [^1]: Mufradat of Raghib. [^2]: Al-Mizan and Fi Zilal al-Quran (under the verses discussed). [^1]: Refer to verses 134 and 135 of Al-A'raf, 5 of Saba, 11 of Al-Jathiyah, 59 of Al-Baqarah, 162 of Al-A'raf, and 34 of Al-Ankabut. [^2]: In Tafsir al-Kabir, this meaning is mentioned as a possibility (Vol. 30, p. 193). [^3]: Note that "tastakthir" here is a circumstantial clause (Hal), not the result of the prohibition (as it appears in the nominative case). Thus, the concept of the verse becomes: "Do not confer favor while you seek increase or while you consider your deed as great." [^1] and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 454 and Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 400. [^3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, 82. [^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 29, p. 120. [^2]: Regarding the syntax of this sentence, several possibilities have been given, the best of which is in the book Al-Bayan fi Gharib I'rab al-Quran, which says "dhalika" is the subject (Mubtada), "yawma'idhin" is an appositive (Badal), and "yawmun 'asir" is its predicate (Khabar).

Ayahs

ذَرْنِى وَمَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيدًا (١١)
Leave Me [to deal] with him whom I created alone, (11)
وَجَعَلْتُ لَهُۥ مَالًا مَّمْدُودًا (١٢)
and furnished him with extensive means, (12)
وَبَنِينَ شُهُودًا (١٣)
[gave him] sons to be at his side, (13)
وَمَهَّدتُّ لَهُۥ تَمْهِيدًا (١٤)
and facilitated [all matters] for him. (14)
ثُمَّ يَطْمَعُ أَنْ أَزِيدَ (١٥)
Still he is eager that I should give him more. (15)
كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا عَنِيدًا (١٦)
No indeed! He is an obstinate opponent of Our signs. (16)
سَأُرْهِقُهُۥ صَعُودًا (١٧)
Soon I will overwhelm him with hardship. (17)

Circumstances of Revelation

Two accounts for the circumstances of revelation have been mentioned for these verses:

1- The Quraysh gathered in "Dar al-Nadwa" (a center near al-Masjid al-Haram where they assembled to consult on important matters). "Walid" (a well-known and prominent man of Mecca in whose intellect and wisdom the polytheists believed, and whom they consulted on significant issues) turned to them and said: "You are a people of high lineage and intellect, and Arabs come to you from every direction (to visit the Kaaba and otherwise) and they hear different responses from you; unify your word."

Then he turned to them and asked: "What do you say about this man (referring to the Holy Prophet)?" They said: "We say he is a 'poet'!" Walid frowned and said: "We have heard much poetry, but his speech bears no resemblance to poetry." They said: "We say he is a 'soothsayer' (kahin)." He said: "When you go to him, you do not find in him the speech that soothsayers utter (in the form of occult news)." They said: "We say he is 'possessed' (mad)." He said: "When you go to him, you will find no trace of madness in him."

They said: "We say he is a 'sorcerer' (sahir)." He asked: "In what sense a sorcerer?" They said: "One who creates enmity between enemies and friends." He said: "Yes, he is a 'sorcerer' and he does such things!" (Because some of them become Muslims and separate their path from others).

Then they left "Dar al-Nadwa," and whenever any of them met the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they would say: "O sorcerer! O sorcerer!"

This weighed heavily on the Prophet. Allah revealed the opening verses of this Surah and the above verses (up to verse 25) and comforted His Prophet.

2- Some have also said: When the verses of Surah Ha-Mim al-Sajdah (Surah Ghafir) were revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was standing in al-Masjid al-Haram (in prayer), and "Walid bin Mughirah" was near him listening to his recitation. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله noticed this, he repeated the recitation of these verses.

"Walid" went to the assembly of his people—the tribe of Bani Makhzum—and said: "By Allah, I have just heard speech from Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله that resembles neither the speech of humans nor the speech of Jinn. 'Indeed, it has a sweetness, and indeed, it has a grace; its top is fruitful, and its bottom is overflowing; it surely rises high and nothing rises above it!': His speech has a particular sweetness, and a special beauty and freshness; its branches are full of fruit, and its roots are strong and powerful. It is a speech that surpasses every other speech, and no speech shall surpass it!" He said this and returned to his home. The Quraysh said to one another: "By Allah, he has become infatuated with the religion of Muhammad and has left our religion, and he will lead all of Quraysh astray." They used to call Walid "Rayhanat Quraysh" (the flower of the Quraysh tribe).

Abu Jahl said: "I will handle this matter." He set out and came to sit beside Walid with a sorrowful face.

"Walid" asked: "Why are you sorrowful, my nephew?" He replied: "The Quraysh are finding fault with you at your age, and they think you have beautified the speech of Muhammad." He stood up with Abu Jahl and entered the assembly of the people, saying: "Do you think Muhammad is mad? Have you ever seen traces of madness in him?" They said: "No."

He asked: "Do you think he is a soothsayer? Have you ever seen traces of soothsaying in him?"

They said: "No."

He asked: "Do you imagine he is a poet? Have you ever seen him utter poetry?"

They said: "No."

He asked: "Then do you think he is a liar? Do you have any record of a lie regarding him?" They said: "No, he has always been known among us as 'the Truthful and Trustworthy' (al-Sadiq al-Amin) before his claim to prophethood."

At this point, the Quraysh said to "Walid": "Then in your opinion, what should we say about him?"

Walid thought and looked, frowned, and said: "He is only a sorcerer. Have you not seen how he causes separation between a man and his family, his children, and his friends?"

(A group would believe in him and separate from their families).

"Therefore, he is a sorcerer, and what he says is an impressive sorcery!" [1]

Commentary

"Walid," That Arrogant and Ungrateful Wealthy Man!

Following the previous verses that warned the disbelievers collectively, the verses under discussion specifically point a finger at certain individuals among them who were more influential, placing them under a barrage of the severest warnings with eloquent, expressive, and crushing terms.

First, He says: "Leave Me [to deal] with him whom I created alone" (ذَرْنِي وَ مَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيداً). This verse and the subsequent ones, as mentioned in the circumstances of revelation, were revealed regarding Walid bin Mughirah al-Makhzumi, one of the famous leaders of the Quraysh.

The term "wahidan" (alone/unique) could be a description for the Creator or for the created. In the first case, there are two possibilities: first, "Leave me alone with him so that I Myself may punish him severely," or "I alone created him and bestowed all these blessings upon him, yet he was ungrateful." In the second case, there are also two possibilities: it may refer to the fact that he was all alone in his mother's womb and at birth, possessing neither wealth nor children, all of which I granted him later; or it refers to the fact that he called himself "Wahid" (the unique individual among the Arabs), and it is famous that he would say: "I am the unique son of the unique one; there is no peer for me among the Arabs, nor was there a peer for my father!" [1] This matter is repeated in the verse as a form of mockery.

However, among all these four interpretations, the first interpretation is the most appropriate.

Then He adds: "And I granted him extensive and abundant wealth" (وَ جَعَلْتُ لَهُ مالًا مَمْدُوداً).

"Mamduud" originally means "extended," which here refers to the vastness and volume of his wealth, or its extension in terms of time or place.

Some have said his wealth was so extensive that he possessed many camels, numerous horses, and vast real estate between Mecca and Ta'if.

Others have said he had gardens and farms such that before the harvest of one ended, the next would arrive; additionally, he possessed one hundred thousand gold dinars. All these meanings are gathered in the word "mamduud."

Then, referring to the abundance of his human strength, He adds: "And sons [always] present [with him and in his service]" (وَ بَنِينَ شُهُوداً). They were constantly ready to help and serve, and their presence was a source of companionship and comfort for him. They were never forced to travel to distant places due to financial hardship, leaving their father in the pain of distance, separation, and loneliness. In some narrations, it is stated he had ten sons.

And then, referring generally to the other blessings He had bestowed upon him, He says: "And I smoothed [the way] for him [in every respect]" (وَ مَهَّدْتُ لَهُ تَمْهِيداً). Not only wealth and prominent sons, but in social aspects and physical dimensions, he was immersed in blessings from every angle.

"Tamheed" is derived from the root "mahd," which originally means the place prepared for a child (a cradle and the like). Subsequently, it was applied to any means of comfort, progress, position, and prominent social status. Overall, it has a broad meaning that includes various blessings of life and means of progress and success.

But instead of bowing in humility before the Bestower of all these blessings and rubbing his forehead on His threshold, he turned to ingratitude and greed: "Yet he greedily desires that I should add more" (ثُمَّ يَطْمَعُ أَنْ أَزِيدَ). This was not exclusive to "Walid bin Mughirah"; rather, all world-worshippers are like this. Their thirst is never quenched, and if the seven realms were placed under their signet, they would still be in bondage to another realm.

However, the next verse sternly rejects this unworthy person, saying: "By no means! Indeed, he has been stubborn toward Our signs" (كَلَّا إِنَّهُ كانَ لِآياتِنا عَنِيداً).

Despite knowing well that this Quran is neither the speech of Jinn nor the speech of man, that it has powerful roots, fruitful branches, and an unparalleled attraction, he still called it "sorcery" and its bringer a "sorcerer!" "Anid" is derived from "anad," which some say refers to that type of opposition and enmity that is carried out consciously—meaning a person perceives the truth of something and yet rises in opposition to it. "Walid" was a clear manifestation of this meaning.

The use of "kana" (has been) indicates that his stubbornness against the truth was a continuous and permanent matter, not something occasional or fleeting.

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, He refers to his painful fate with a short and meaningful phrase, saying: "I will soon compel him to climb a difficult slope [of life, and then I will cast him down from its peak]" (سَأُرْهِقُهُ صَعُوداً).

"Sa-urhiquhu" is from the root "irhaq," which originally means "covering something by force," and it has also come to mean imposing difficult tasks and afflicting with various types of punishment.

"Sa'ud" (with the weight of kabud) means a place that is climbed, and "su'ud" (with the weight of qu'ud) means "climbing."

Since climbing high peaks is a very arduous and difficult task, this expression is used for any difficult and laborious work. Therefore, some have interpreted it as divine punishment, and some have said "Sa'ud" is a mountain of fire in Hell that he is forced to climb, or it is a difficult mountain with a steep slope such that when he climbs it, he falls and crashes down, and this matter is constantly repeated.

The possibility also exists that the verse refers to the worldly punishment of "Walid" in this life; for as recorded in history, after reaching the peak of success in his personal and social life, he fell so hard that until the end of his life, he continuously lost his wealth and children and became wretched. [1]

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 386. This circumstance of revelation has been narrated by many commentators such as "Qurtubi," "Maraghi," "Fakhr Razi," "Fi Zilal al-Quran," "Al-Mizan," and others (with variations). [^1]: Tafsir "Fakhr Razi," "Kashshaf," "Maraghi," and "Qurtubi" under the verses discussed. It is also understood from some narrations that "Wahid" means fatherless or illegitimate, but there is no evidence in this narration that it is the interpretation of the above verse; furthermore, upon careful examination, it becomes clear that this meaning is not consistent with the verse above. [^1]: "Tafsir Maraghi," Vol. 29, p. 131.

Ayahs

إِنَّهُۥ فَكَّرَ وَقَدَّرَ (١٨)
Indeed he reflected and decided. (18)
فَقُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ (١٩)
Perish he, how he decided! (19)
ثُمَّ قُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ (٢٠)
Again, perish he, how he decided! (20)
ثُمَّ نَظَرَ (٢١)
Then he looked; (21)
ثُمَّ عَبَسَ وَبَسَرَ (٢٢)
then frowned and scowled. (22)
ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ (٢٣)
Then he walked away disdainfully, (23)
فَقَالَ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ (٢٤)
saying, ‘It is nothing but traditional sorcery. (24)
إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا قَوْلُ ٱلْبَشَرِ (٢٥)
It is nothing but the speech of a human.’ (25)

Commentary

Death be upon him, what an evil plot he devised!

In these verses, further details are provided regarding the man to whom Allah had granted abundant wealth and children, yet who rose in opposition to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله—namely, "Walid ibn al-Mughirah" al-Makhzumi.

It says: "Indeed, he thought about what he should accuse the Prophet and the Quran of,

and he prepared the matter in his mind" (إِنَّهُ فَكَّرَ وَ قَدَّرَ).

It is evident that thinking is inherently a good act, provided it is on the path of Truth; sometimes one hour of such thought holds the virtue of a year's worship, or even a lifetime, because that single hour can entirely transform a person's destiny. However, if thought is employed in the path of disbelief, corruption, and devilry, it is reprehensible and condemned, and Walid's thinking was of this very kind.

"Qaddara" (from the root taqdir) here means preparing the matter in the mind and deciding on the execution of that evil plot.

Then, to condemn him, He adds: "Woe to him! How he plotted to combat the Truth" (فَقُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ).

Then, as an emphasis, He adds: "Again, woe to him! How he prepared the matter"

to combat the Truth (ثُمَّ قُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ).

This refers to what was mentioned in the occasion of revelation: that he wanted to unite the thoughts of the polytheists and give them a single direction, so they would propagate a single claim against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله with one voice. When they suggested giving the Prophet the title of "poet," he did not accept. Then they suggested the title of "soothsayer," but he did not agree. Afterward, the title of "madman" was suggested, which he also did not like. Finally, they suggested calling the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله a "sorcerer," and he agreed to it. This was because, in his imagination, the effect of sorcery was to cause separation between two friends and create friendship between two opposing individuals, and this phenomenon had appeared with the emergence of Islam and the Quran. Since this plan and plot were prepared after study and reflection, the Quran describes it with the phrase "فَكَّرَ وَ قَدَّرَ" (he thought and plotted), which is very brief yet profound. Thus, even if the suggestion came from others, the thought and selection were carried out by "Walid."

In any case, this sentence, especially considering its repetition, is evidence that he possessed skill in satanic thoughts, such that his thinking and reflection were a cause for wonder.

Then He adds: "Then he looked again" (ثُمَّ نَظَرَ). He re-examined what his mind had fashioned and prepared to ensure its necessary strength, cohesion, and various aspects.

"Then he frowned and acted hastily" (ثُمَّ عَبَسَ وَ بَسَرَ).

"Then he turned his back to the Truth and acted arrogantly" (ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ وَ اسْتَكْبَرَ).

"And finally he said: This is nothing but an enchanting sorcery like that which has been narrated from the ancients" (فَقالَ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ).

"This is only the word of a human" and has no connection to heavenly revelation! (إِنْ هذا إِلَّا قَوْلُ الْبَشَرِ). In this way, he expressed his final word after repeated study and satanic reflection to combat the Quran. Despite all the devilry that this "thinker" of the era of ignorance and polytheism exerted, he—without realizing it—extraordinarily praised and extolled the Quran with this statement. He showed that the Quran possesses an extraordinary attraction that conquers the heart of every human being, and according to him, there is an enchanting influence in it that mesmerizes hearts. Since the Quran bore no resemblance to the sorcery of magicians, but rather consisted of logical, rhythmic, and calculated words, this itself was proof that it is not the word of a human and has originated from a heavenly revelation, from the metaphysical world, and from the infinite knowledge of Allah, gathering all beauties alongside cohesion and strength within itself.

"Abasa" is from the root abus (on the pattern of julus), meaning to frown or contract the face, and abus (on the pattern of majus) refers to one who possesses this quality.

"Basara" is from the root busur and basr (on the pattern of nasr), which sometimes means rushing to do something before its time, and sometimes means scowling and altering the facial expression.

In the verse under discussion, if the second meaning is intended, it harmonizes with the phrase "abasa."

If it carries the first meaning, it refers to his agitation and hasty decision-making in choosing an incorrect "label" for the Quran.

The word "yu'thar" is from the root athar, meaning a narration passed down from the ancients or remains left by them. Some have considered it to be from the root ithar, meaning to choose and give preference.

According to the first meaning, Walid says: "This is sorcery like the sorceries narrated from the ancients." According to the second meaning, he says: "It is sorcery that, due to its sweetness and attraction, affects the hearts, and people prefer it over everything else."

In any case, this is an implicit confession to the miraculous nature of the Quran, for the Quran has no similarity to the supernatural acts of sorcerers. It is a firm and solid discourse, full of spirituality, and possessing unparalleled attraction and influence. If, as Walid claimed, it were the word of a human, others should also have been able to bring its like. Yet we know that the Quran repeatedly issued a challenge, and not one of the fierce enemies who possessed extraordinary skill in the Arabic language could bring anything equal to it, nor even less than it—and this is the meaning of a miracle.

Ayahs

سَأُصْلِيهِ سَقَرَ (٢٦)
Soon I will cast him into Saqar. (26)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا سَقَرُ (٢٧)
And what will show you what is Saqar? (27)
لَا تُبْقِى وَلَا تَذَرُ (٢٨)
It neither spares, nor leaves [anything]. (28)
لَوَّاحَةٌ لِّلْبَشَرِ (٢٩)
It burns the skin. (29)
عَلَيْهَا تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ (٣٠)
There are nineteen [keepers] over it. (30)

Commentary

And This is His Ill-Fated Destiny

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 25; p. 232

Continuing the previous verses which recounted the condition of some of the leaders of polytheism and their words in denying and rejecting the Holy Qur'an and the mission of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), these verses point to his terrifying punishment on the Day of Resurrection.

It says: "Soon I will cast him into Hell and burn him in the fire of Saqar!" (سَأُصْلِيهِ سَقَرَ).

"Saqar" (سقر) is originally derived from the root saqar (rhyming with faqr), meaning to change and melt due to the heat of the sun. Subsequently, it was chosen as one of the names of Hell and appears repeatedly in the verses of the Qur'an. The choice of this name points to the horrific torments of Hell that seize its inhabitants. Some have also considered it the name of one of the terrifying levels or depths of Hell.

Then, to express the magnitude and intensity of the punishment of Hell, it says: "And what will make you know what Saqar is?" (وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا سَقَرُ). That is, its punishment is so severe that it lies outside the circle of imagination and cannot be contained by anyone's thought, just as the significance and greatness of the heavenly blessings do not occur to anyone's mind.

"It leaves nothing remaining, and it spares nothing!" (لَا تُبْقِي وَلَا تَذَرُ). This sentence may refer to the fact that the fire of Hell—unlike the fire of this world, which sometimes affects one part of the body while another remains safe, or sometimes affects the body while the soul remains secure—is an all-encompassing fire that envelops the entire existence of a human being and leaves nothing behind.

Some have also said the meaning is that it neither causes the inhabitants of Hell to die nor keeps them alive; rather, they are constantly trapped between death and life, as stated in verse 13 of Surah Al-A'la: لَا يَمُوتُ فِيهَا وَلَا يَحْيَىٰ "He will neither die therein nor live!"

Or it means that it leaves neither skin nor flesh on the body, nor does it leave the bone intact. Therefore, the concept of the verse is not that it completely incinerates and annihilates them [into non-existence], because this meaning is not consistent with what is stated in verse 56 of Surah An-Nisa, where He says: كُلَّمَا نَضِجَتْ جُلُودُهُم بَدَّلْنَاهُمْ جُلُودًا غَيْرَهَا لِيَذُوقُوا الْعَذَابَ "Every time their skins are roasted through, We will replace them with other skins so they may taste the punishment!"

Then, proceeding to describe another characteristic of this blazing fire of Divine wrath, it adds: "It completely transforms the skin, visible to humans from a great distance" (لَوَّاحَةٌ لِّلْبَشَرِ) [1]. It makes the face so black and dark that it is darker than a pitch-black night.

"Bashar" (بشر) here is the plural of basharah, meaning the outer surface of the skin, and it could also mean "human beings." "Lawwahah" (لواحة) is derived from the root lawh, which sometimes means to appear and become manifest, and sometimes means to change and transform.

According to the first meaning, the interpretation of the verse would be: Hell becomes visible to humans from a long distance, as stated in verse 36 of Surah An-Nazi'at: وَبُرِّزَتِ الْجَحِيمُ لِمَن يَرَىٰ "And Hell shall be made manifest for him who sees."

According to the second meaning, the interpretation is: "Hell completely transforms the color of the skin."

And in the last verse under discussion, He says: "Nineteen [angels of punishment] are appointed over it" (عَلَيْهَا تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ) [1]. These are angels who are certainly not commanded to show mercy, compassion, or kindness, but are commanded to inflict retribution, punishment, and severity.

Although only the number nineteen is mentioned in the above verse and it does not explicitly state "angels of punishment," it is clearly understood from the following verse that this number refers to the count of the angels who are the agents of punishment.

Some have also said it refers to nineteen groups of angels, not nineteen individuals, and they consider the phrase "And none knows the soldiers of your Lord except Him" (وَمَا يَعْلَمُ جُنُودَ رَبِّكَ إِلَّا هُوَ) which appears in the next verse as evidence for this meaning.

But why, among all numbers, was the number nineteen chosen for the agents of Divine punishment? No one knows exactly for certain, but some have speculated that it is because the number nineteen combines the largest single-digit number (nine) and the smallest double-digit number (ten).

Others have said it is because the roots of vile morals trace back to nineteen internal and external traits, and since each vile moral is a factor for Divine punishment, the levels of Hell are nineteen, corresponding to them, and in each level, an angel or a group of angels is commissioned for punishment.

Fundamentally, matters related to the Resurrection, Paradise, Hell, and their details and specifics are not precisely clear to us who are prisoners in the limited environment of this world. What we know are the generalities. Therefore, it is mentioned in narrations that each of these nineteen angels possesses such immense power that they could easily cast a large tribe into Hell.

From here, the weakness and feebleness of the thoughts of those who think like Abu Jahl becomes clear. When he heard this verse, he mockingly said to the tribe of Quraysh: "May your mothers mourn you! Do you not hear what 'Ibn Abi Kabshah' (referring to the Holy Prophet [1]) says? He says the keepers of Hell are nineteen! Yet you are a great group of brave men; can every ten of you not overcome one of them?!" Abu al-Asad al-Jumahi (one of the strongmen of Quraysh) said: "I will take care of seventeen of them for you, and you handle the other two!" [2]. These shallow-minded people wanted to block the light of Truth with such mockery and save themselves from certain destruction.

[1] "Lawwahah" is the predicate (khabar) of a hidden subject, estimated as "hiya lawwahah" (it is lawwahah). [1] In this sentence, "alayha" is the advanced predicate and "tis'ata 'ashara" is the delayed subject. We know this word is indeclinable (mabni) on fatha, and thus it does not outwardly take the nominative case (raf'); the reason given is that it implies the meaning of the conjunction "and" (waw). [1] Regarding why the Quraysh called the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) by this title, some have said: It is because there was a man from the tribe of Khuza'ah named "Abu Kabshah" who abandoned the worship of idols during the Age of Ignorance. Since the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was strongly opposed to idol worship, they called him the son of "Abu Kabshah." Others said "Abu Kabshah" was one of the maternal ancestors of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). In any case, there is no doubt that their intention in choosing this name was mockery, as "Kabsh" in the Arabic language means "ram," although this title has also been used in praise to refer to commanders and the brave of an army. [2] "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 388, and other commentaries.

Key Point: The Number Nineteen is the Number of the Angels of Punishment

The above verses clearly introduce the number of the keepers of Hell as nineteen individuals, or nineteen groups and armies, and the subsequent verses also emphasize this point. However, the strange thing is that some deviant sects insist on the sanctity of this number, and even try to organize the number of months in a year and the number of days in a month around this axis of nineteen, contrary to all natural and astronomical standards! They have even based their practical laws upon it.

Even stranger is that a writer, who perhaps is not unrelated to their organization, has a strange and ridiculous insistence on justifying everything in the Qur'an based on this number nineteen. In many cases where this desired number does not harmonize with the existing realities of the Quranic verses, he adds or subtracts something at his own whim so that it aligns with the number nineteen or multiples of nineteen. Spending time mentioning their claims and responding to them would perhaps be a waste of time.

Indeed, a "Hellish religion" must revolve around a "Hellish number," and a Hellish group must harmonize itself with the number of the angels of punishment!

Ayah

وَمَا جَعَلْنَآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلنَّارِ إِلَّا مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً ۙ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا عِدَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِيَسْتَيْقِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَيَزْدَادَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنًا ۙ وَلَا يَرْتَابَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۙ وَلِيَقُولَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ مَاذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا مَثَلًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَا يَعْلَمُ جُنُودَ رَبِّكَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَمَا هِىَ إِلَّا ذِكْرَىٰ لِلْبَشَرِ (٣١)
We have assigned only angels as keepers of the Fire, and We have made their number merely a stumbling block for the faithless, and so that those who were given the Book may be reassured, and the faithful may increase in [their] faith, and so that those who were given the Book and the faithful may not be in doubt, and so that the faithless and those in whose hearts is a sickness may say, ‘What did Allah mean by this description?’ Thus does Allah lead astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes. No one knows the hosts of your Lord except Him, and it is just an admonition for all humans. (31)

Commentary

What is the Purpose of This Number of Hell’s Officials?

As we read in the preceding verses, Allah mentions the number of the keepers and officials of Hell as nineteen (or nineteen groups). We also read that the mention of this number sparked discussion among the polytheists and disbelievers; some took it as an object of ridicule, while a small group perceived this low number as an indication that overcoming them would not be a difficult task! The verse under discussion, which is the longest verse of this Surah, responds to them and clarifies more truths in this regard.

First, He says: "And We have not made the keepers of the Fire (the officials of punishment) except angels" (وَ ما جَعَلْنا أَصْحابَ النَّارِ إِلَّا مَلائِكَةً) [^1]. These are angels who are mighty, powerful, and in the words of the Quran, "stern" and "severe" (ghiladh and shidad), harsh and rigorous, such that all sinners are weak and helpless before them.

Then He adds: "And We have not made their number except as a trial for those who disbelieve" (وَ ما جَعَلْنا عِدَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

This trial was from two aspects: First, they mocked why the number 19 was chosen out of all numbers, whereas had any other number been chosen, the same question could have been raised.

[^1]: The expression "Ashab al-Nar" (Companions of the Fire) is mentioned many times in the verses of the Holy Quran, and everywhere it means "the dwellers of Hell," except here where it means "the keepers of Hell." Furthermore, the use of this expression regarding them indicates that "Saqar" in the previous verses refers to the entirety of Hell, not just a specific part of it.

On the other hand, they considered this number small and said mockingly: "We will pit ten people against each one of them to crush them!" This is while the angels of God are such that, according to the Quran, a few of them were commissioned to destroy the people of Lot, lifting their prosperous cities from the earth and turning them upside down. Moreover, the selection of the number nineteen for the keepers of Hell involves points that were explained in the preceding verses.

He further adds: "[The goal was] that those who were given the Scripture (Jews and Christians) might reach certainty" (لِيَسْتَيْقِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ). It is mentioned in a narration that a group of Jews asked some of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله about the number of the keepers of Hell. He replied that Allah and His Messenger know best. Gabriel descended at that very moment and informed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "عَلَيْها تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ" (Over it are nineteen [individuals or groups]) [^1]. Since they did not object to this, it became clear that they found it consistent with their own scriptures, and their certainty in the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله increased. This matter also caused the believers to become firmer in their creed and faith; therefore, in the next phrase He adds: "And [the goal was] that those who believe may increase in faith" (وَ يَزْدادَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِيماناً). Immediately after mentioning this phrase, He returns to those same three goals for emphasis, once again stressing the faith of the People of the Book, then the believers, and then

[^1]: This Hadith has been narrated by "Bayhaqi," "Ibn Abi Hatim," and "Ibn Mardawayh" from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 29, p. 134).

the trial of the disbelievers and polytheists, saying: "And [the goal was] that those who were given the Scripture and the believers may not doubt, and that those in whose hearts is a disease and the disbelievers may say: 'What does Allah intend by this description (parable)?'" (وَ لا يَرْتابَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ وَ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَ لِيَقُولَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَ الْكافِرُونَ ما ذا أَرادَ اللَّهُ بِهذا مَثَلًا) [^1].

Regarding who is meant by "الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ" (those in whose hearts is a disease), some have said it refers to the "hypocrites," as this expression is used for them in the verses of the Quran. For instance, in verse 10 of Surah Al-Baqarah, which speaks about the hypocrites based on the context of the preceding and succeeding verses, we read: فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَهُمُ اللَّهُ مَرَضاً: "In their hearts is a disease, so Allah has increased their disease." They have taken this as evidence that the above verse is Medinan, because the group of hypocrites emerged when Islam gained power in Medina, not in Mecca.

However, an analysis of the instances where this expression is mentioned in the Quranic verses shows that it is not exclusive to the hypocrites; rather, it is applied to all disbelievers who possessed a state of obstinacy, stubbornness, and enmity toward the verses of truth. Sometimes it is even mentioned alongside the hypocrites, which may indicate they are two distinct groups. For example, in verse 49 of Surah Al-Anfal, we read: إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَ الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ غَرَّ هؤُلاءِ دِينُهُمْ: "When the hypocrites and those in whose hearts was a disease said, 'Their religion has deluded these people (the Muslims).'" There are other similar verses as well.

[^1]: It should be noted that the "Lam" in "ليستيقن" is the "Lam of Cause" (Lam al-Illah), and in "ليقول" it is the "Lam of Consequence" (Lam al-Ghayah). Perhaps for this reason it was repeated, whereas if it were for the same meaning, its repetition would not have been necessary. In other words, the believers attaining certainty is Allah's will and command, but the blasphemous words of the disbelievers are not His command; rather, they were the consequence of this matter.

Therefore, there is no reason to deny that this verse is Meccan, especially since it has complete harmony and connection with the preceding verses, in which the signs of being Meccan are fully apparent (observe carefully).

Then, following these discussions about how the believers and the diseased-hearted disbelievers benefit from the divine words, He adds: "Thus Allah leaves astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills." (كَذلِكَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ).

The preceding sentences clearly show that this divine will and volition regarding the guidance of some and the straying of others is not arbitrary. Those who are obstinate, stubborn, and diseased-hearted deserve nothing else, and those who are submissive and believing before Allah's command deserve such guidance.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And none knows the soldiers of your Lord except Him, and this is not but a reminder for humanity!" (وَ ما يَعْلَمُ جُنُودَ رَبِّكَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَ ما هِيَ إِلَّا ذِكْرى‌ لِلْبَشَرِ).

Therefore, if there is talk of nineteen keepers of Hell, it does not mean that Allah's soldiers are limited to these; rather, the soldiers of the Lord are so numerous that, according to some narrations, they have filled the entire earth and heavens. Indeed, "there is no space for a footstep in the entire universe except that an angel is there occupied with the glorification of Allah!"

Regarding what the pronoun "هى" (it/this) in the phrase "وَ ما هِيَ إِلَّا ذِكْرى‌ لِلْبَشَرِ" (and this is not but a reminder for humanity) refers to, commentators have offered several possibilities:

Sometimes they have said: It refers to the "soldiers and armies of God," a group of whom are the keepers of Hell. Sometimes they have said: It refers to "Saqar," meaning Hell itself. And sometimes it is said: It refers to the verses of the Holy Quran.

Although each of these is a source of reminder, awakening, and awareness, the first interpretation seems more appropriate given the tone of the verses. The goal is to express the truth that if Allah has chosen soldiers and armies for Himself, it is not because He Himself cannot punish all the obstinate and sinful; rather, these are for the purpose of reminding, awakening, and drawing attention to the seriousness of the issue of divine punishment.

Key Point: The Number of the Lord's Soldiers!

Allah's presence everywhere and the extension of His power throughout the entire universe make His Holy Essence independent of any helper, assistant, or army. Nevertheless, to demonstrate His greatness to the creation and for their reminder and recollection, He has chosen numerous soldiers and armies who execute His command throughout the world.

In Islamic narrations, extraordinary expressions have been recorded regarding the multitude, greatness, and power of the Lord's soldiers. Since these do not fit the scales we are accustomed to, hearing them is very astonishing to us.

Here, we suffice with the magnificent words narrated from Ali عليه السلام in the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha in this regard. Since the passage is long, we provide only the translation:

"Then He opened the high heavens and filled them with various classes of angels: Some of them are always in prostration and do not perform bowing (ruku). Some are constantly in bowing and do not raise their heads. Some are permanently standing, occupied with worship, and do not change their position. Some constantly glorify Him and do not tire. Sleep never covers their eyes, nor does error or forgetfulness overcome their intellects. Their bodies do not become weak, nor does negligence or oblivion affect them. Another group are the trustees of His revelation and His tongue to the Prophets, who are constantly coming and going to convey His decree and command. Another group are the protectors of His servants and the gatekeepers of the lofty Paradise. Some of them have their feet fixed in the lower layers of the earth, while their necks have passed beyond the highest heaven! Their existential pillars have gone beyond the dimensions of the world, and their shoulders are ready to preserve the foundations of the Throne of God." [^1]

As we have said before, "Malak" (Angel) has a broad and expansive meaning that includes both angels who possess intellect, consciousness, obedience, and submission, as well as many of the forces and powers of the universe.

We have provided a more detailed explanation of this subject in the commentary on the opening verses of Surah Fatir (Vol. 18, p. 165 onwards).

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 1.

Ayahs

كَلَّا وَٱلْقَمَرِ (٣٢)
No indeed! By the Moon! (32)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذْ أَدْبَرَ (٣٣)
By the night when it recedes! (33)
وَٱلصُّبْحِ إِذَآ أَسْفَرَ (٣٤)
By the dawn when it brightens! (34)
إِنَّهَا لَإِحْدَى ٱلْكُبَرِ (٣٥)
Indeed they are one of the greatest [signs of God] (35)
نَذِيرًا لِّلْبَشَرِ (٣٦)
—a warner to all humans, (36)
لِمَن شَآءَ مِنكُمْ أَن يَتَقَدَّمَ أَوْ يَتَأَخَّرَ (٣٧)
[alike] for those of you who like to advance ahead and those who would remain behind. (37)

Commentary

In continuation of the discussion with the deniers of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the denial of the Resurrection, these verses swear several oaths and emphasize the issue of the Day of Judgment, the Resurrection, Hell, and its punishment.

He says: "It is not as they imagine; by the moon" (كَلَّا وَ الْقَمَرِ).

"Kalla" (كَلَّا) is a particle of "deterrence" (rad'a), and is usually used to negate words previously mentioned by the opposing party, and sometimes to negate subsequent words. Here, based on the context of the preceding verses, it negates the delusions of the polytheists who denied Hell and its punishment and mocked the number of the angels who are the keepers of Hell.

The mention of the oath by the "moon" is because it is one of the great signs of Allah, both in terms of its creation and its regular orbit, as well as its light, beauty, and gradual changes, which serve as a living calendar for determining the days.

Then He adds: "And by the night when it retreats and withdraws its skirt" (وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذْ أَدْبَرَ).

"And by the morning when it removes the veil from its face" (وَ الصُّبْحِ إِذا أَسْفَرَ) [1]. In fact, these three oaths are related to one another and complement each other, for we know that the moon's manifestation occurs during the nights, and the light of the moon during the day is overshadowed by the light of the sun and has no manifestation at all.

[1]: "Asfara" (أَسْفَرَ) is derived from the root "safar" (on the pattern of faqr), meaning to open a covering or to remove a veil; hence, women who do not wear a veil are called "safirat." This expression regarding the dawning of the morning contains a beautiful and interesting metaphor.

The night, although it is tranquil and silent, and a time for the intimate prayers of the lovers of Truth, is most interesting when it retreats and moves toward the bright morning at the end of the dawn. The dawning of the morning, which marks the end of the dark night, is the most beautiful and soul-stirring of all, bringing joy and vitality to every human being and immersing them in light and purity.

These three oaths also bear a resemblance to the light of the "Quranic" guidance, the retreating of the darkness of "polytheism" and idol-worship, and the dawning of the morning of "Monotheism" (Tawhid).

After stating these oaths, He addresses the subject for which the oaths were taken, saying: "Surely the terrifying events of the Resurrection, Hell, and the angels of punishment are among the great matters" (إِنَّها لَإِحْدَى الْكُبَرِ). [1] The pronoun in "innaha" (إِنَّها) refers either to "Saqar" (Hell), or the "hosts" and armies of the Lord, or the collection of the events of the Resurrection; whichever it may be, its greatness is clear.

Then He adds: "The purpose of creating Hell is never for revenge; rather, it is a means for warning mankind" (نَذِيراً لِلْبَشَرِ). [2]

[1]: "Kubar" (كُبَرِ) is the plural of "kubra," meaning great. Some have said the meaning is that Saqar is one of the great levels of Hell, but this interpretation is not consistent with what we previously mentioned and what is understood from the verses.

[2]: "Nadhiran" (نَذِيراً) is a circumstantial adverb (hal) for the pronoun in "innaha" which refers back to "Saqar," and some have taken it as a "tamyiz" (specification), which would be the case if "nadhir" had an infinitive meaning and meant "warning" (indhar), but the first possibility seems more correct.

So that it may warn everyone and caution them against the terrifying punishment that awaits the disbelievers, the sinners, and the enemies of the Truth.

Finally, for further emphasis, He adds: This warning is not specific to a certain group, but rather "it is for all of mankind; for those of you who wish to advance and move forward toward good deeds and obedience to Allah's command, and those who wish to lag behind this caravan" (لِمَنْ شاءَ مِنْكُمْ أَنْ يَتَقَدَّمَ أَوْ يَتَأَخَّرَ). Whoever chooses the path of "advancement," glad tidings be to him, and whoever treads the path of "lagging behind" and regression, woe unto him.

Some have also suggested the possibility that "advancing" and "lagging behind" here refer to advancing toward the fire of Hell or staying back from it.

Others have said it refers to the advancement of the human soul and its perfection, or its degradation and lagging behind.

The first and third meanings are more appropriate, while the second interpretation is not very fitting.

كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ (٣٨)
Every soul is hostage to what it has earned, (38)
إِلَّآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٣٩)
except the People of the Right Hand. (39)
فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٤٠)
[They will be] in gardens, questioning (40)
عَنِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٤١)
the guilty: (41)
مَا سَلَكَكُمْ فِى سَقَرَ (٤٢)

قَالُوا۟ لَمْ نَكُ مِنَ ٱلْمُصَلِّينَ (٤٣)
They will answer, ‘We were not among those who prayed. (43)
وَلَمْ نَكُ نُطْعِمُ ٱلْمِسْكِينَ (٤٤)
Nor did we feed the poor. (44)
وَكُنَّا نَخُوضُ مَعَ ٱلْخَآئِضِينَ (٤٥)
We used to indulge in [profane] gossip along with the gossipers, (45)
وَكُنَّا نُكَذِّبُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٤٦)
and we used to deny the Day of Retribution (46)
حَتَّىٰٓ أَتَىٰنَا ٱلْيَقِينُ (٤٧)
until death came to us.’ (47)
فَمَا تَنفَعُهُمْ شَفَـٰعَةُ ٱلشَّـٰفِعِينَ (٤٨)
So the intercession of the intercessors will not avail them. (48)

Commentary

Why Did You Become Inhabitants of Hell?

Following the discussion regarding Hell and its inhabitants in the preceding verses, these verses add: "Every soul is a pledge for what it has earned" (كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ).

"Rahinah" (رهينة) is derived from the root "Rahn" (رهن), meaning "hostage" or "pledge." It refers to a security usually given in exchange for a "loan." It is as if the entire existence of a human being is held in pledge for the fulfillment of their duties and obligations; when they perform them, they are released, otherwise, they remain in the bonds of captivity.

From some linguistic sources, it appears that one of the meanings of "Rahn" is "constant accompaniment" [1]. According to this meaning, the concept of the verse would be that everyone is accompanied by their own deeds, whether they are righteous or evildoers.

However, based on the context of the subsequent verses, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Therefore, it immediately states: "Except the companions of the right, who are free from the bonds of this captivity" (إِلَّا أَصْحابَ الْيَمِينِ). In the light of faith and righteous deeds, they have broken the shackles of captivity and enter Paradise without an accounting [2]. There is a discussion among commentators as to who is meant by "Companions of the Right" (أَصْحابَ الْيَمِينِ) here:

Some have interpreted it as those whose record of deeds is given to their right hand.

Others consider them to be believers who have no sins at all, and some have considered it a reference to the angels, among other possibilities.

However, what emerges from various verses of the Quran and has Quranic evidence is the first meaning: they are those who possess faith and righteous deeds, and if they have minor sins, those are overshadowed by their good deeds, and by the decree of:

[1]: Lisan al-Arab, root "Rahn".

[2]: Regarding whether the "exception" here is "connected" (muttasil) or "disconnected" (munqati'), some, like the late Sheikh Tusi in Al-Tibyan, have taken it as "disconnected," while others, like the author of Ruh al-Bayan, consider it "connected." This difference relates to the various interpretations mentioned regarding the concept of "Rahinah." According to the interpretation we have chosen, the exception is disconnected, while according to the other interpretation, it would be connected (reflect upon this).

"Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds" (Hud 114).

Their good deeds cover their bad deeds; they either enter Paradise without an accounting, or if there is an accounting, it is easy and simple, as stated in verse 7 of Surah Al-Inshiqaq: "Then as for him who is given his record in his right hand, he will be judged with an easy account" (فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَسَوْفَ يُحاسَبُ حِساباً يَسِيراً).

Qurtubi, the famous Sunni commentator, narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse that he said: "We and our Shi'a are the 'Companions of the Right,' and whoever hates us, the Ahl al-Bayt, they are the ones held in pledge [by their deeds]" [1]. Other commentators, including those in Majma' al-Bayan and the author of Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, have cited this hadith under the verses in question.

Then, touching upon a portion of the state of the "Companions of the Right" and the opposing group, it adds: "They will be in gardens [of Paradise], inquiring..." (فِي جَنَّاتٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ) [2]

"About the criminals" (عَنِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ).

They say: "What led you into Saqar (Hell)?" (ما سَلَكَكُمْ فِي سَقَرَ). From these verses, it is clearly understood that the connection between the people of Paradise and the people of Hell is not completely severed; the people of Paradise can observe the condition of the people of Hell from their realm and engage in conversation with them.

Now let us see what the criminals say in response to this question from the "Companions of the Right." They confess to four of their major sins in this regard:

First: "They will say, 'We were not of those who prayed'" (قالُوا لَمْ نَكُ مِنَ الْمُصَلِّينَ).

Had we prayed, our prayer would have reminded us of Allah, forbidden us from indecency and evil, and invited us to the Straight Path of Allah.

Second: "Nor did we used to feed the poor" (وَ لَمْ نَكُ نُطْعِمُ الْمِسْكِينَ). Although "feeding the poor" literally means giving food to the destitute, it apparently refers to any kind of assistance for the essential needs of the needy, whether it be food, clothing, housing, or otherwise.

As commentators have clarified, this refers to the "obligatory Zakat," because neglecting recommended (mustahabb) charity does not cause one to enter Hell. This verse once again emphasizes that Zakat had been revealed in general terms in Mecca, even though the legislation of its details, the determination of its limits, and especially its centralization in the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) took place in Medina.

Third: "And we used to indulge in vain discourse with those who indulged" (وَ كُنَّا نَخُوضُ مَعَ الْخائِضِينَ). Whenever a voice was raised from any corner against the truth, or any gathering was formed to promote falsehood and we became aware of it, we would join them and conform to the crowd. We would confirm their words, validate their denial and rejection, and take pleasure in their mockery and ridicule of the truth.

"Nakhuwdhu" (نخوض) is from the root "Khawdh" (خوض), which originally means entering and moving in water, and was later applied to entering and becoming defiled by other matters. In the Holy Quran, it is mostly used for entering into false and baseless topics.

"Indulging in falsehood" has a broad meaning that includes attending gatherings of those who mock the signs of Allah, spread anti-Islamic propaganda, promote innovations (bid'ah), engage in foul jokes, or recount the sins they have committed with pride or pleasure. It also includes participating in gatherings of backbiting, slander, and vain amusement. However, in the verse under discussion, the focus is primarily on gatherings formed to undermine the religion of Allah, mock sacred things, and promote disbelief, polytheism, and irreligion.

Then they add: "And we used to deny the Day of Recompense" (وَ كُنَّا نُكَذِّبُ بِيَوْمِ الدِّينِ).

"Until there came to us the certainty (death)" (حَتَّى أَتانَا الْيَقِينُ).

It is clear that denying the Resurrection and the Day of Account and Recompense destabilizes all divine and moral values and encourages a person to commit sins, removing the obstacles from this path—especially if this denial becomes a continuous state until the end of life.

In any case, from these verses, it is clearly understood that, firstly, disbelievers are obligated to the branches of religion (furu' al-din) just as they are obligated to the principles of religion (usul al-din). It also shows that these four matters—namely "prayer," "zakat," "avoiding the gatherings of the people of falsehood," and "faith in the Resurrection"—have an extraordinary importance and role in the guidance and cultivation of a human being. Thus, Hell is not the place for true performers of prayer, givers of zakat, avoiders of falsehood, and believers in the Resurrection.

Of course, since prayer is the worship of Allah, it is not possible without faith in Him; therefore, its mention is a symbol for faith and belief in Allah and submission to His command. It can be said that these four matters begin with Monotheism (Tawhid) and end with the Resurrection (Ma'ad), encompassing the human's relationship with the "Creator," "Creation," and "Self."

It is well-known among commentators that "Certainty" (Yaqin) here means death, as it is a certain matter for both the believer and the disbeliever; a person may doubt anything, but they cannot doubt death. In verse 99 of Surah Al-Hijr, we also read: "And worship your Lord until there comes to you the certainty (death)" (وَ اعْبُدْ رَبَّكَ حَتَّى يَأْتِيَكَ الْيَقِينُ).

However, some have interpreted "Certainty" here as the awareness obtained after death regarding the matters of the Purgatory (Barzakh) and the Resurrection, which is consistent with the first interpretation in one respect.

In the final verse under discussion, it points to the grim end of this group, saying: "So the intercession of the intercessors will not benefit them," and they must remain in divine punishment forever (فَما تَنْفَعُهُمْ شَفاعَةُ الشَّافِعِينَ).

Neither the intercession of the Prophets and Messengers of the Lord and the Imams, nor the intercession of the angels, the truthful (Siddiqin), the martyrs, and the righteous; for intercession requires the existence of a receptive ground, and these people have completely destroyed those grounds. Intercession is like pure water poured at the base of a weak sapling; obviously, if the sapling is completely dead, this pure water will not revive it. In other words, as we have stated in the discussion on intercession, "Shafa'ah" (intercession) is from the root "Shaf'" (شفع) meaning "joining one thing to another." The concept is that the person being interceded for has traveled part of the way on their own feet but has become stranded in difficult terrains; the intercessor joins them and helps them to travel the rest of the way [1]. Furthermore, this verse once again emphasizes the issue of intercession and the diversity and multiplicity of intercessors in the presence of Allah, providing a crushing response to those who deny the principle of intercession.

It is also an emphasis that intercession is not unconditional and is not considered a "green light" for sin; rather, it is a factor for human cultivation to at least bring them to a stage where they are eligible for intercession, and their connection with Allah and His friends is not completely severed.

[1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 1, under verse 48 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

It is also worth mentioning that the concept of the above verse is not that the intercessors will rise to intercede for such people; rather, its concept is that since intercession would not benefit them, they do not intercede, for the friends of Allah (Awliya Allah) do not engage in futile or vain actions.

Key Points The Intercessors of the Day of Recompense

From the verses under discussion, as well as some other verses of the Holy Quran, it is understood that the intercessors on the Day of Resurrection are multiple (and the scope of their intercession varies). From the collection of narrations cited from both Shi'a and Sunni sources—and these narrations are very numerous—it is understood that these intercessors will intercede on the Day of Resurrection for sinners in whom the grounds for intercession exist.

  1. The first intercessor is the person of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, as we read in a hadith where the Messenger of Allah said: "I am the first intercessor in Paradise" [1].

  2. All the Prophets are among the intercessors of the Day of Resurrection, as stated in another hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "The Prophets will intercede for everyone who sincerely bears witness that there is no god but Allah, and they will bring them out of it [Hell]" [2].

[1]: Sahih Muslim, Vol. 2, p. 130.

[2]: Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 3, p. 12.

  1. The angels are among the intercessors of the Day of Gathering, as narrated from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: "Permission will be given to the angels, the Prophets, and the martyrs to intercede" [1].

4 and 5. The Infallible Imams and their Shi'a also intercede, as Ali عليه السلام says: "For us there is intercession, and for those who love us there is intercession" [2].

6 and 7. Scholars and thinkers, as well as the martyrs in the way of Allah, are among the intercessors, as we read in a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "On the Day of Resurrection, first the Prophets will intercede, then the scholars, then the martyrs" [3]. It is even stated in a hadith from the same Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that: "The intercession of a martyr will be accepted for seventy people from his household" [4]. In another hadith cited by the late Allamah Majlisi in Bihar al-Anwar: "Their intercession will be accepted for seventy thousand people!" [5]. Given that the numbers seventy and seventy thousand are both used to denote multiplicity, there is no contradiction between these two narrations.

  1. The Quran also intercedes on the Day of Resurrection, as Ali عليه السلام said: "And know that it (the Quran) is an intercessor whose intercession is accepted" [1].

  2. Those who have spent a lifetime in Islam, as we read in a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "When a man reaches ninety years of age (and continues on the path of faith), Allah forgives his past and future sins, and his intercession for his family is accepted" [2].

  3. Acts of worship also intercede, as stated in a hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: "Fasting and the Quran will intercede for the servant on the Day of Resurrection" [3].

  4. It is understood from some narrations that good deeds, like a trust that a person has strived to protect, also intercede for the person on the Day of Resurrection [4].

  5. Interestingly, it is understood from some narrations that Allah Himself also intercedes for sinners, as stated in another hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "The Prophets, the angels, and the believers will intercede, and the Almighty (al-Jabbar) will say: 'My intercession remains'" [5].

[2]: Al-Khisal by Saduq, p. 624.

[3]: Sunan Ibn Majah, Vol. 2, p. 1443.

[4]: Sunan Abi Dawud, Vol. 2, p. 15.

[5]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 100, p. 14.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 176.

[2]: Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 2, p. 89.

[3]: Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 2, p. 174.

[4]: Manaqib Ibn Shahr Ashub, Vol. 2, p. 14.
