The narrations in this field are very numerous, and what we have mentioned is only a portion of them [1]. We repeat again that intercession has conditions and constraints without which it is not possible, as explicitly stated in the verses under discussion that for a group of criminals, the intercession of all intercessors is ineffective. The important thing is that the capacity of the recipient must also exist, for the agency of the actor alone is not sufficient. (We have provided more explanation in this regard in Volume 1 under the discussion of intercession).

[1]: For further explanation, you may refer to the book Mafahim al-Quran, Vol. 4, pp. 288 to 311.

Ayahs

فَمَا لَهُمْ عَنِ ٱلتَّذْكِرَةِ مُعْرِضِينَ (٤٩)
What is the matter with them that they evade the Reminder (49)
كَأَنَّهُمْ حُمُرٌ مُّسْتَنفِرَةٌ (٥٠)
as if they were terrified asses (50)
فَرَّتْ مِن قَسْوَرَةٍۭ (٥١)
fleeing from a lion? (51)
بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُؤْتَىٰ صُحُفًا مُّنَشَّرَةً (٥٢)
But everyone of them desires to be given unrolled scriptures [from Allah]! (52)
كَلَّا ۖ بَل لَّا يَخَافُونَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ (٥٣)
No! Indeed, they do not fear the Hereafter. (53)
كَلَّآ إِنَّهُۥ تَذْكِرَةٌ (٥٤)
No! It is indeed a reminder. (54)
فَمَن شَآءَ ذَكَرَهُۥ (٥٥)
So let anyone who wishes be mindful of it. (55)
وَمَا يَذْكُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ هُوَ أَهْلُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ وَأَهْلُ ٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ (٥٦)
And they will not be mindful unless Allah wishes. He is worthy of [your] being wary [of Him] and He is worthy to forgive. (56)

Commentary

They Flee from the Truth Like Zebras from a Lion!

Continuing the discussion regarding the fate of the criminals and the inhabitants of Hell in the previous verses, the verses under discussion reflect the terror of this antagonistic and stubborn group upon hearing the word of truth and any form of admonition or advice in the clearest possible way.

First, It says: "Why are they so averse to the reminder?"

(فَما لَهُمْ عَنِ التَّذْكِرَةِ مُعْرِضِينَ‌) ^1 Why do they flee from the healing medicine of the Quran? Why do they reject the compassionate physician? Truly, it is a cause for wonder.

"As if they were startled and fleeing zebras" (كَأَنَّهُمْ حُمُرٌ مُسْتَنْفِرَةٌ).

^1: In this sentence, "ma" is the "subject" (mubtada), "lahum" is the "predicate" (khabar), "mu'ridin" is the "circumstantial adverb" (hal) for the pronoun in "lahum," and "an al-tadhkirah" is a "preposition and object" (jarr wa majrur) related to "mu'ridin." Some believe that placing "an al-tadhkirah" before "mu'ridin" implies restriction (hasr), meaning they only turn away from beneficial reminders. In any case, "tadhkirah" here refers to any useful and beneficial reminder, at the head of which stands the Holy Quran.

"That have fled from a lion (or a hunter)" (فَرَّتْ مِنْ قَسْوَرَةٍ). "Humur" is the plural of "himar," meaning donkey; however, here, by the context of fleeing from the claws of a lion or a hunter, it refers to wild donkeys or zebras ^1. In other words, this word has a broad meaning that includes both wild and domestic ones.

"Qaswarah" is derived from the root "qasr" (meaning force and dominance) and is one of the names for a "lion." Some have also interpreted it to mean an archer or a hunter, but the first meaning seems more appropriate here.

It is well known that the "zebra" has an extraordinary fear of the "lion." Even when it hears the lion's roar, such terror overcomes it that it runs frantically in every direction. Especially when a lion reaches a herd of them, they scatter and run so wildly that it amazes any observer. Fundamentally, this animal, by its wild nature, is terrified of everything, let alone its bloodthirsty enemy, the lion.

In any case, this verse is a very eloquent and expressive description of the polytheists' terror and flight from the soul-nurturing verses of the Quran. It likens them to zebras that lack intellect and reason and, due to their wild nature, flee from everything, while before them lies nothing but a "reminder" (a means of awakening and awareness).

Yet, despite all this ignorance and unawareness, they are so pretentious and arrogant that each of

them expects that separate letters be given to him from Allah!" (بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ يُؤْتى‌ صُحُفاً مُنَشَّرَةً) ^1 This is similar to what appears in Surah Al-Isra, verse 93: "And we will never believe in your ascension until you bring down to us a book that we may read." Or Surah Al-An'am, verse 124, which says:

"They say: We will never believe until we are given the like of what was given to the messengers of Allah!" In this way, each of them expected to be an Arch-Prophet (Ulul-'Azm) and for a specific letter in their name to be sent down from heaven by Allah. Even if such a thing were sent down, it is not certain they would believe.

It is mentioned in some narrations that Abu Jahl and a group from the Quraysh said: "O Muhammad! We will not believe in you until you bring a letter from heaven addressed: 'From Allah, Lord of the Worlds, to so-and-so, son of so-and-so,' and in it, we are formally commanded to believe in you!" [^2]

Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "No! It is not as they say" (كلا). The demand for a heavenly book to be sent down to them and other such matters are all mere excuses.

^1: "Suhuf" is the plural of "sahifah," meaning a leaf or page that is turned over (since the root "sahf" and "sahifah" means something spread out like a face); it was later applied to letters and books.

[^2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Tafsir al-Maraghi, and other commentaries.

"In truth, they do not fear the Hereafter" (بَلْ لا يَخافُونَ الْآخِرَةَ).

If they feared the punishment of the Hereafter, they would not make all these excuses, they would not deny the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, they would not mock the divine verses, and they would not make the number of angels appointed for punishment a subject of ridicule.

From here, the effect of belief in the Resurrection on piety (taqwa), abstaining from sin, and purification from various major transgressions becomes clear.

It must be rightly said that belief in the world of Resurrection and the rewards and punishments of Judgment Day grants a person a new personality. It can transform reckless, arrogant, selfish, and oppressive individuals into committed, pious, humble, and just human beings.

Then, He emphasizes once more that it is not as they think regarding the Quran (كلا). "Indeed, the Quran is a reminder" (إِنَّهُ تَذْكِرَةٌ).

"And whoever wills may take heed from it" (فَمَنْ شاءَ ذَكَرَهُ‌). The Quran has both shown the way and provided the seeing eye and the sunlight, "so that man may look before his own feet."

At the same time, taking heed from it is not possible except by the Will and success (tawfiq) granted by Allah: "And they will not take heed except if Allah wills" (وَ ما يَذْكُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللَّهُ‌). This sentence has several interpretations. One is what we mentioned above: that man

cannot traverse the path of guidance unless he seeks recourse in the grace of the Lord and asks Him for success and assistance. Unless there is an attraction from the Beloved, The effort of the helpless lover leads nowhere. And of course, this divine assistance descends upon fertile grounds.

Another interpretation is: the sentence "And whoever wills may take heed from it" in the previous verse might create the illusion that everything depends solely on man's own will, and that his will is independent in every respect. This verse removes this misconception by stating that man, while being possessed of choice and free will, is dependent on the Will of Allah—a Will that governs the entire universe and the world of creation. In other words: this very choice and freedom of man also exist by His Will, and He can take it away whenever He wishes.

A third interpretation is: it says they will not believe at any cost unless Allah wills and forces them, and we know that Allah does not force anyone into belief or disbelief.

Of course, the first and second interpretations are better and more appropriate.

At the end of the verse, He says: "He is the Source of Piety and the Source of Forgiveness" (هُوَ أَهْلُ التَّقْوى‌ وَ أَهْلُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ).

It is fitting that they fear His punishment and abstain from associating anything with Him, and it is also fitting that they hope for His forgiveness.

In reality, this sentence is an allusion to the stations of "fear" (khawf) and "hope" (raja'), and divine "punishment" and "forgiveness." It is, in fact, a justification for the previous verse.

Thus, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of this verse that he said:

"Allah says: 'I am worthy of being feared, so that My servant does not associate anything with Me; and I am worthy, if My servant does not associate anything with Me, to admit him into Paradise.'" ^1

Although all commentators, as far as we have seen, have interpreted "Taqwa" here in the "passive" sense (maf'uli) and said Allah is worthy that one should be pious toward Him (avoiding polytheism and sin against Him), there is also the possibility that it be interpreted in the "active" sense (fa'ili). That is, Allah Himself is the "Possessor of Taqwa"; He abstains from any injustice, ugliness, or any act contrary to wisdom. In reality, the highest station of Taqwa belongs to Allah, and what exists in His servants is but a faint spark of that infinite Taqwa, even though the term "Taqwa" in the active sense is less commonly used regarding Allah.

In any case, this Surah began with the command to "warn" and "duty" and ends with the invitation to "piety" and the "promise of forgiveness." It is here that with the utmost humility and supplication, we present to His holy presence:

O Lord! Place us among the people of Your piety and forgiveness. O Allah! Unless Your success and favors take our hand, we shall reach nowhere; include us in this grace.

O God! The trap is difficult, the path is winding, and Satan is ready to seduce; traversing this path is not possible except by Your aid. Aid us.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Muddaththir, 19th of Safar, 1407.

^1: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 405.
